images.caption:
Figure 1:We presentSWE-chat, a continually growing dataset of real human-coding agent interactions collected from public GitHub repositories.
Developers opt in via installingEntire.io, an open-source tool that automatically logs coding agent sessions and links them to code commits with line-level human vs. agent attribution.
As of April 2026,SWE-chatcontains 2.7M logged events from 200+ repositories, including 63,000+ user prompts and 355,000+ tool calls.

Figure 2:Usage patterns and failure modes inSWE-chat.
Using theSWE-chatdataset, we analyzehow people use coding agents in the wild(left) andwhen and how they fail(right).
Text colorings correspond to figure components.
Results reflect theSWE-chatpopulation of open-source developers using public repositories and opting into session logging.
Figure 3:Structure of a coding agent session inSWE-chat.Each session consists of alternating user prompts and agent responses with tool calls (file reads, edits, shell commands) and text output.

Figure 4:SWE-chatuser-agent interaction statistics.
(a) Distribution of turns per session.
(b) Distribution of agent tool calls per turn. (c) Top 15 file types touched by agent tool calls.

header: 3How do humans interact with coding agents in the wild? (RQ1)

images.caption: Figure 5:Vibe coding in the wild.% of agent-authored code, structured into three coding modes:human-only(0% agent-authored code),collaborative(0–99%), andvibe coding(≥\geq99%).

header: 4How do coding agents fail and how do users respond? (RQ2)

images.caption: Figure 6:Distribution of LLM-annotated session success rating.The distribution is left-skewed, indicating that most sessions are rated as largely successful.
Figure 7:Cost efficiency per 100 committed lines of code.μ\muindicates means.

Figure 8:Turn-level oversight in Claude Code sessions. Fraction of turns in which the agent stops to ask for clarification, the user interrupts the agent, or the user pushes back against the agent’s response—broken down by coding mode.
Figure 19:Distributions of human user intents (a), agent tool calls (b), repository domains (c), repository audiences (d), and coding modes (e).
Figure 20:Top 6 prompt languages.
(a)Tool call composition by position within the agent trajectory (left to right).
(a)Tool call composition by position within the agent trajectory (left to right).
(b)Tool call composition counting from the end of natural (non-interrupted) turns.
(c)Tool call composition counting from the end of interrupted turns.

Figure 22:Cumulative fraction of sessions originating from theentireio/clirepository over time.
Each point shows the running proportion of all sessions collected up to that date that came from this single repository.
The dashed red line marks the public launch of the Entire.io tool (February 10, 2026).

Figure 23:Topic distribution of user prompts.
Each bar represents one of the 20 clusters identified by HDBSCAN*, labeled with a GPT-generated topic summary.
The remaining panels show, per cluster, pushback rate, agent turn duration in seconds, and session success score distribution for sessions in which at least 20% of prompts fall within the cluster.
Several clusters (1, 8, 10, 15, 20) contain a disproportionate amount of prompts originating
from Entire.io’s ownrepository.
Figure 24:Distribution of user personas.
Figure 25:14-day rolling average of coding modes and agent-authored code.
Figure 26:Distribution of introduced vulnerabilities across Semgrep rule IDs (top 15 plusother).
Figure 27:Distribution of introduced vulnerabilities across CWE categories (top 15 plusother).

Figure 29:Token, cognitive, and time efficiency per 100 committed lines of code (lower is better).
Y-axis labels describe the metrics used for the efficiency dimension.μ\muindicates means.

Figure 30:Turn-level autonomy in Claude Code sessions. Agent turn duration in interactive Claude Code sessions. Showing 7-day rolling average of different percentiles (p50, p90, p99, p99.9) over time.
Figure 31:Agent stops for clarification, user interruptions, and soft user pushback over time.
Figure 32:Agent behavior by development activity (code writing vs. code reviewing).
Figure 33:Inter-annotator agreement confusion matrices.
Figure 34:LLM annotation agreement with gold labels from human expert annotations.
